comments.text: J. Ma, T. Yang [Upper bounds on the extremal number of the 4-cycle, Bull. Lond. Math. Soc. 55 (2023), no. 4, 1655-1667.] proved that There exist some real $\epsilon > 0$ and a positive density of integers $n$ such that $$ex(n, C_4)\leq \frac{1}{2}n^{\frac{3}{2}}+ (\frac{1}{4}- \epsilon)n.$$ See also J.A. Bondy and U.S.R. Murty [ Graph Theory with Applications (Macmillan, New York, 1976).], p.247, Problem 8. The survey article on this Erdos conjecture can be found in F.R.K,Chung [Open problems of Paul Erdos in graph theory, J. Graph Theory 25 (1997), 3-36.] Z.Furedi, M. Simonovits [The history of degenerate (bipartite) extremal graph problems. Erdos centennial, 169-264, Bolyai Soc. Math. Stud., 25, Janos Bolyai Math. Soc., Budapest, 2013.] J. Verstraete [Extremal problems for cycles in graphs, Recent trends in combinatorics, 83-116, IMA Vol. Math. Appl., 159, Springer, Cham, 2016]. C. Lai, M. Liu[Some open problems on cycles, Journal of Combinatorial Mathematics and Combinatorial Computing 91 (2014), 51-64. MR3287706]

The sequence $(c_1,c_2,\cdots,c_n)$ is the cycle length distribution of a graph $G$ of order $n$， where $c_i$ is the number of cycles of length $i$ in $G$. Let $f(a_1,a_2,\cdots,$ $a_n)$ denote the maximum possible number of edges which satisfies $c_i\leq a_i$ for all $1\leq i \leq n $, where $a_i$ is a nonnegative integer. Given a graph $H$, the Tur\'an number $ex(n, H)$ is defined as the maximum number of edges in an $n$-vertex graph not containing $H$ as a subgraph. Denote by $m_i(n)$ the numbers of cycles of length $i$ in the complete graph $K_n$ on $n$ vertices. Then \begin{align*} ex(n,C_{k})&=f(0,0,m_3(n), \cdots,m_{k-1}(n),0,m_{k+1}(n), \cdots,m_n(n))\\ &=f(0,0,2^{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}},\cdots,2^{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}}, 0,2^{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}}, \cdots, 2^{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}}). \end{align*} Thus $ex(n,C_k)$ is a special case of $f(a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n)$. also see Chapter 1,2 In book: Some Problems on Paths and Cycles https://www.researchgate.net/publication/392767794_Some_Problems_on_Paths_and_Cycles

Building on the work of [BoSi74], J. Verstraete in [Ve00] improved the bound to $\mathrm{ex}(n, C_{2k}) ≤ 8(k − 1)n^{1+1/k}.$ Pikhurko in [Pi12] improved the bound further to $\mathrm{ex}(n, C_{2k}) ≤(k − 1)n^{1+1/k} + O(n).$ Bukh and Jiang in [BuJi14] have improved the bound to $\mathrm{ex}(n, C_{2k}) ≤ 80 \sqrt{k} \log k · n^{1+1/k} + 10k^2n$ Z. He in [He21] has improved the bound to $\mathrm{ex}(n, C_{2k}) \leq (16 \sqrt{5} \sqrt{k \log k}+o(1))· n^{1+1/k}$
